questions.question:
Let L_1 and L_2 be any two context-free languages and R be any regular language. Then which of the following is/are CORRECT?

I. L_1 union L_2 is context-free
II. L_1 intersect L_2 is context-free
III. L_1 - R is context-free
IV. R - L_1 is context-free

A. I, II and IV only
B. I and III only
C. II and IV only
D. I only
questions.to_find: Which closure properties hold for context-free languages

understand.plain: We are given two context-free languages L1 and L2, and a regular language R. We need to check which operations on them always produce a context-free language. CFLs are closed under union and concatenation, but NOT under intersection or complement. However, CFLs are closed under intersection with regular languages.

keywords.explain: A language generated by a context-free grammar (CFG) or accepted by a pushdown automaton (PDA).
A class of languages is closed under an operation if applying that operation to languages in the class always yields a language still in the class.
keywords.example: Regular languages are closed under complement; CFLs are not.

keywords.explain: The intersection of a CFL with a regular language is always a CFL. This is a special closure property.
keywords.example: {a^n b^n | n >= 1} intersect a*b* = {a^n b^n | n >= 1}, still CFL

understand.visual_alt: Table of closure properties: CFLs are closed under union (yes), intersection (no), complement (no), difference with regular (yes), regular minus CFL (no).

given.aim: Determine which of the four operations on CFLs and regular languages always yield a CFL

terms.example: {a^n b^n} and {a^n b^n c^n} are CFLs (the second is actually not CFL, but hypothetically)
terms.connects: Used in union and intersection operations
Used in set difference operations with CFLs

terms.meaning: Union of two CFLs is always a CFL (proved by combining their CFGs)
terms.example: L1 union L2 can be handled by a new start symbol S -> S1 | S2

given.plan: Check each statement against known closure properties of CFLs: (I) CFLs closed under union -> TRUE; (II) CFLs NOT closed under intersection -> FALSE; (III) L1 - R = L1 intersect complement(R), and complement of regular is regular, and CFL intersect regular = CFL -> TRUE; (IV) R - L1 = R intersect complement(L1), complement of CFL is not necessarily CFL, and this could be non-CFL -> FALSE.

steps.title: Check Statement I: L1 union L2 is context-free
steps.apply: CFLs are closed under union. Given grammars G1 for L1 and G2 for L2, we create a new grammar with start symbol S and rules S -> S1 | S2. So L1 union L2 is always a CFL. Statement I is TRUE.
steps.note: This is one of the fundamental closure properties of CFLs.

steps.title: Check Statement II: L1 intersect L2 is context-free
steps.apply: CFLs are NOT closed under intersection. Classic counterexample: L1 = {a^n b^n c^m | n,m >= 1} and L2 = {a^m b^n c^n | n,m >= 1} are both CFLs, but L1 intersect L2 = {a^n b^n c^n | n >= 1} which is not a CFL. Statement II is FALSE.
steps.note: Intersection of two CFLs can produce a non-CFL (even a non-context-free language).

steps.title: Check Statement III: L1 - R is context-free
steps.apply: L1 - R = L1 intersect complement(R). Since R is regular, complement(R) is also regular. The intersection of a CFL with a regular language is always a CFL (proved using PDA+DFA product construction). So L1 - R is always a CFL. Statement III is TRUE.
steps.note: This is a key property: CFL intersect Regular = CFL.

steps.title: Check Statement IV: R - L1 is context-free
steps.apply: R - L1 = R intersect complement(L1). The complement of a CFL is not necessarily a CFL (CFLs are NOT closed under complement). So R - L1 need not be a CFL. Counterexample: Let L1 = {a^n b^n c^n | n >= 0} intersected... actually let's take L1 = {w | w is not of the form a^n b^n} (complement of {a^n b^n} is a CFL... let's reconsider). More directly: R - L1 = complement(L1) intersect R. If R = Sigma*, then R - L1 = complement(L1). Since CFLs are not closed under complement, R - L1 need not be CFL. Statement IV is FALSE.
steps.note: The key asymmetry: CFL intersect Regular is CFL, but Regular - CFL (= Regular intersect complement(CFL)) need not be CFL.

steps.title: Combine results
steps.apply: Statements I and III are TRUE; II and IV are FALSE. The answer is 'I and III only'.
steps.note: Answer is option B.

questions.formula_note: Key closure properties of CFLs: closed under union and concatenation, intersection with regular languages; NOT closed under intersection, complement, or set difference with another CFL.

questions.question:
The set of all recursively enumerable languages is:

A. closed under complementation
B. closed under intersection
C. a subset of the set of all recursive languages
D. an uncountable set
questions.to_find: Which property holds for the class of recursively enumerable (RE) languages

understand.plain: Recursively enumerable (RE) languages are those accepted by a Turing machine (which may loop forever on non-accepted inputs). We need to identify which given property is true for the class of all RE languages. RE languages are closed under union and intersection, but NOT under complementation.

keywords.explain: A language accepted by some Turing machine. The TM may halt and accept, halt and reject, or loop forever.
keywords.example: The halting problem language is RE but not recursive.

keywords.explain: A language for which there exists a Turing machine that always halts and correctly accepts or rejects every input.
keywords.example: All regular, CFL, and context-sensitive languages are recursive.

understand.visual_alt: Chomsky hierarchy: Regular subset CFL subset Recursive (decidable) subset RE. RE is not closed under complement; recursive languages are.

given.aim: Identify which closure or membership property holds for the class of all RE languages

terms.meaning: Union of two RE languages is RE
terms.example: Run two TMs in parallel; accept if either accepts
terms.connects: Hints that intersection closure may also hold

terms.meaning: Intersection of two RE languages is RE
terms.example: Run two TMs simultaneously; accept only if both accept

terms.meaning: The complement of an RE language may not be RE
terms.example: The halting problem is RE; its complement is not RE

given.plan: Verify each option: (A) RE is not closed under complementation - FALSE; (B) RE is closed under intersection - TRUE; (C) RE languages include non-recursive languages, so RE is NOT a subset of recursive - FALSE; (D) The set of all languages is uncountable, but the set of RE languages (each described by a TM, which is a finite string) is countably infinite - FALSE.

steps.title: Check option A: RE closed under complementation
steps.apply: RE is NOT closed under complementation. The halting problem H = {<M,w> | M halts on w} is RE. Its complement (TMs that do not halt on w) is not RE. So option A is FALSE.
steps.note: This distinguishes RE from recursive languages; recursive IS closed under complementation.

steps.title: Check option B: RE closed under intersection
steps.formula_raw: L1 intersect L2 accepted by product TM
steps.apply: If L1 is accepted by TM M1 and L2 by TM M2, we can build a TM M that simulates M1 and M2 in parallel (dovetailing) and accepts w only if both M1 and M2 accept w. So L1 intersect L2 is RE. Option B is TRUE.
steps.note: The key is that if both TMs eventually accept, our combined TM will also accept in finite time.

steps.title: Check option C: RE is a subset of recursive languages
steps.apply: RE languages include non-recursive (undecidable) languages like the halting problem. Recursive languages are a PROPER SUBSET of RE. So RE is not a subset of recursive. Option C is FALSE.
steps.note: The hierarchy is: Recursive (proper subset of) RE.

steps.title: Check option D: RE is an uncountable set
steps.apply: Each RE language is accepted by some Turing machine. Turing machines are described by finite strings over a finite alphabet, so the set of all TMs is countably infinite. Therefore the set of RE languages is at most countably infinite. Option D is FALSE.
steps.note: The set of ALL languages over {0,1} is uncountable (2^aleph_0), but RE languages form only a countable subset.

questions.formula_note: RE languages are closed under union, intersection, concatenation, and Kleene star, but NOT under complementation. Recursive languages are closed under all these plus complementation.

questions.question:
Consider the two lists List I and List II given below:

List I:
(i) Context-free languages
(ii) Recursive languages
(iii) Regular languages

List II:
(a) Closed under union
(b) Not closed under complementation
(c) Closed under intersection

For matching of items in List I with those in List II, which of the following option(s) is/are CORRECT?

A. (i) - (a), (ii) - (b), and (iii) - (c)
B. (i) - (b), (ii) - (a), and (iii) - (c)
C. (i) - (b), (ii) - (c), and (iii) - (a)
D. (i) - (a), (ii) - (c), and (iii) - (b)
questions.to_find: Correct matchings of language classes with their closure properties

understand.plain: We need to match three language classes (CFL, Recursive, Regular) with three properties: closed under union, not closed under complementation, closed under intersection. Multiple options may be correct as this is an MSQ question. Regular languages are closed under all Boolean operations. CFLs are closed under union but NOT under intersection or complementation. Recursive languages are closed under all Boolean operations including complementation.

keywords.explain: If L1 and L2 are in the class, then L1 union L2 is also in the class.
keywords.example: Regular, CFL, and Recursive languages are all closed under union.

keywords.explain: If L1 and L2 are in the class, then L1 intersect L2 is also in the class.
keywords.example: Regular and Recursive languages are closed under intersection; CFL is NOT.

keywords.explain: If L is in the class, then complement(L) is also in the class.
keywords.example: Regular and Recursive languages are closed under complement; CFL is NOT.

understand.visual_alt: Table showing closure properties: Regular languages are closed under union, intersection, and complement (all yes). CFL is closed under union only (intersection and complement are no). Recursive languages are closed under all three.

given.aim: Match language classes to their distinguishing closure properties

terms.connects: Closed under union (a) but NOT under intersection or complementation; (b) 'not closed under complementation' is true for CFL

terms.example: All regular and context-free languages are recursive
terms.connects: Closed under union (a), intersection (c), and complementation; (b) is FALSE for recursive

terms.meaning: Accepted by finite automata; closed under all Boolean operations
terms.connects: Closed under both union (a) and intersection (c)

given.plan: Identify valid matchings: CFL -> (a) closed under union is TRUE; CFL -> (b) not closed under complementation is TRUE. Recursive -> (a) closed under union is TRUE; Recursive -> (c) closed under intersection is TRUE; Recursive -> (b) is FALSE (recursive IS closed under complement). Regular -> (a), (c) are both TRUE; (b) is FALSE. Then check each option.

steps.title: Establish closure properties for each class
steps.apply:
Regular: closed under union (a) YES, intersection (c) YES, complementation YES -> (b) 'not closed under complementation' is FALSE for regular.
CFL: closed under union (a) YES, intersection (c) NO, complementation NO -> (b) 'not closed under complementation' is TRUE for CFL.
Recursive: closed under union (a) YES, intersection (c) YES, complementation YES -> (b) is FALSE for recursive.
steps.note: Only CFL satisfies 'not closed under complementation'.

steps.title: Evaluate option A: (i)-(a), (ii)-(b), (iii)-(c)
steps.apply: (i) CFL - (a) closed under union: TRUE. (ii) Recursive - (b) not closed under complementation: FALSE (recursive IS closed under complement). (iii) Regular - (c) closed under intersection: TRUE. Since (ii)-(b) is incorrect, option A appears wrong. Wait - let me reconsider. The question says which matchings are CORRECT. Option A claims CFL is closed under union (TRUE), Recursive is not closed under complementation (FALSE), Regular is closed under intersection (TRUE). Since the matching (ii)-(b) is wrong, option A is INCORRECT.
steps.note: Recursive languages ARE closed under complementation, so (ii)-(b) is a wrong match.

steps.title: Evaluate option B: (i)-(b), (ii)-(a), (iii)-(c)
steps.apply: (i) CFL - (b) not closed under complementation: TRUE. (ii) Recursive - (a) closed under union: TRUE. (iii) Regular - (c) closed under intersection: TRUE. All three matchings are correct! Option B is CORRECT.
steps.note: CFL is not closed under complement; Recursive is closed under union; Regular is closed under intersection.

steps.title: Evaluate option C: (i)-(b), (ii)-(c), (iii)-(a)
steps.apply: (i) CFL - (b) not closed under complementation: TRUE. (ii) Recursive - (c) closed under intersection: TRUE. (iii) Regular - (a) closed under union: TRUE. All three matchings are correct! Option C is also CORRECT.
steps.note: Both B and C are valid since each language class is matched to a property it genuinely satisfies.

steps.title: Evaluate option D: (i)-(a), (ii)-(c), (iii)-(b)
steps.apply: (i) CFL - (a) closed under union: TRUE. (ii) Recursive - (c) closed under intersection: TRUE. (iii) Regular - (b) not closed under complementation: FALSE (regular IS closed under complement). Option D is INCORRECT.
steps.note: Regular languages are closed under ALL Boolean operations, so 'not closed under complementation' is wrong for regular.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.apply: Options B and C are both correct. B: CFL-(b), Recursive-(a), Regular-(c). C: CFL-(b), Recursive-(c), Regular-(a).
steps.note: This is an MSQ so multiple options can be correct.

solution.result: B and C (Options B and C are correct)

questions.formula_note: Closure property summary: Regular closed under all Boolean ops. CFL closed under union/concatenation/Kleene star but NOT intersection or complement. Recursive closed under all Boolean ops. RE closed under union/intersection but NOT complement.

questions.question:
Let L be a context-free language and M be a regular language. Then the language L intersect M is:

A. always regular
B. never regular
C. always a deterministic context-free language
D. always a context-free language
questions.to_find: What language class L intersect M belongs to, where L is CFL and M is regular

understand.plain: When we intersect a context-free language with a regular language, the result is always context-free. This is an important closure property. The result may or may not be regular, and it may or may not be deterministic CFL - but it is always at least a CFL.

keywords.explain: The intersection of any CFL with any regular language always produces a CFL. This is proved by running a PDA and DFA simultaneously.
keywords.example: If L = {a^n b^n | n >= 1} (CFL) and M = a*b* (regular), then L intersect M = {a^n b^n | n >= 1} (still CFL).

keywords.explain: A technique to combine a PDA (for CFL) with a DFA (for regular) to build a new PDA that accepts the intersection.
keywords.example: States of new PDA are pairs (q_PDA, q_DFA); transitions follow both machines simultaneously.

keywords.explain: A CFL accepted by a deterministic PDA (DPDA). Not all CFLs are deterministic.
keywords.example: {ww^R | w in {a,b}*} is CFL but not DCFL.

understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram: Regular is a subset of DCFL which is a subset of CFL. CFL intersect Regular = CFL. The result is always in the CFL class but not necessarily in Regular or DCFL.

given.aim: Classify the language L intersect M where L is CFL and M is regular

terms.meaning: A context-free language, accepted by some PDA
terms.connects: We use the PDA for L in the product construction

terms.meaning: A regular language, accepted by some DFA
terms.connects: We use the DFA for M in the product construction

terms.meaning: A PDA constructed from the PDA for L and DFA for M that accepts L intersect M
terms.example: States are (p, q) where p is PDA state and q is DFA state; accept only when both machines accept
terms.connects: Proves that L intersect M is always accepted by a PDA, hence always CFL

given.plan: Use the product construction theorem: given PDA for L and DFA for M, build a product PDA accepting L intersect M. This proves L intersect M is always a CFL. Then check if stronger claims (always regular, always DCFL) hold with counterexamples.

steps.title: Prove L intersect M is always a CFL
steps.formula_raw: Product construction: PDA x DFA = PDA
steps.apply: Let P = (Q_P, Sigma, Gamma, delta_P, q0_P, Z0, F_P) be a PDA for L and D = (Q_D, Sigma, delta_D, q0_D, F_D) be a DFA for M. Construct product PDA P' with states Q_P x Q_D, start state (q0_P, q0_D), accepting states F_P x F_D, and transitions: when P reads symbol a moving from state p to p' (possibly pushing/popping stack symbols), D simultaneously moves from state d to d' = delta_D(d, a). P' transitions correspondingly. P' accepts exactly L intersect M. Since P' is a PDA, L intersect M is a CFL.
steps.note: This is a standard theorem in formal language theory.

steps.title: Show the result is not always regular (eliminate option A)
steps.apply: Take L = {a^n b^n | n >= 1} (CFL, not regular) and M = a*b* (regular). L intersect M = {a^n b^n | n >= 1} which is CFL but not regular. So 'always regular' is false.
steps.note: Option A is incorrect.

steps.title: Show the result is not 'never regular' (eliminate option B)
steps.apply: Take L = {a^n b^n | n >= 1} (CFL) and M = {a} (regular, single string). L intersect M = empty set (or possibly {ab} if n=1 is in M), which is regular. So the result CAN be regular, hence 'never regular' is false.
steps.note: Option B is incorrect.

steps.title: Show the result is not always deterministic CFL (eliminate option C)
steps.apply: Let L = {ww^R | w in {a,b}+} (the palindrome language, which is CFL but not DCFL) and M = (a+b)* (all strings, which is regular). Then L intersect M = L = {ww^R | ...} which is not DCFL. So the result is not always DCFL.
steps.note: Option C is incorrect.

steps.title: Confirm answer
steps.apply: From Step 1, L intersect M is always a CFL. From Steps 2-4, it need not be regular or always DCFL. Therefore the correct answer is D: always a context-free language.
steps.note: This is one of the most important closure theorems for CFLs in GATE.

questions.formula_note: CFL intersect Regular = CFL (proved by product construction of PDA and DFA). This is a fundamental closure property used frequently in GATE problems.
